definition: A combination of medicines that put you in a controlled state of unconsciousness during surgery. You feel no pain and have no memory of the procedure. An anaesthetist monitors you throughout.

A minimally invasive surgical technique using a small camera (laparoscope) inserted through tiny incisions. It allows surgeons to see inside the abdomen without large cuts, resulting in faster recovery and less scarring.
An ultrasound scan of the heart that shows its structure and how well it is pumping blood. It is painless and takes about 45 minutes. Used to diagnose and monitor heart conditions.

A procedure to widen narrowed or blocked coronary arteries. A small balloon is inflated inside the artery and a metal stent is placed to keep it open. Usually performed under local anaesthetic via the wrist.
The surgical replacement of the hip joint with an artificial implant (prosthesis). It is performed to relieve severe arthritis pain and restore mobility. Recovery typically takes 6–12 weeks.

A clinic appointment before planned surgery where nurses check your health, review your medications, take blood samples and explain what to expect. Identifying any issues at this stage improves safety and reduces cancellations.

Your first midwife appointment, usually at 8–10 weeks of pregnancy. The midwife takes a full medical history, arranges blood tests and scans, and gives you information about your care throughout pregnancy.

The process of preparing a patient to leave hospital safely. It includes confirming medication, follow-up appointments, equipment needs and community support. Effective discharge planning reduces re-admissions.
